era_context: The mid-8th century CE spans the height of the Abbasid Caliphate's consolidation of power in the Islamic world, the Tang Dynasty's dominance in East Asia, and the Carolingian Empire's expansion in Western Europe. This period marks significant religious, political, and cultural transformations across Eurasia, with the Abbasid Revolution (750 CE) having recently displaced the Umayyads and begun reshaping Islamic governance and intellectual life.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Sub-Saharan Africa (limited written sources; archaeological record incomplete)
Northern Europe beyond Frankish territories (sparse documentation)
Southeast Asia (limited contemporary chronicles in European archives)

events.title: Abbasid Consolidation and Administrative Reorganization
events.description: The Abbasid Caliphate, having seized power in 750 CE, continues systematic consolidation of authority across the Islamic world. Caliph al-Mansur (r. 754–775 CE) strengthens central administration, establishes the Khorasan Guard as a professional military force, and begins planning the construction of Baghdad as a new capital. This period sees the suppression of remaining Umayyad loyalists and the integration of Persian administrative practices into Islamic governance.
key_figures: Abu Muslim al-Khorasani (military commander, recently deceased 755 CE)

sources.name: Tabari, History of the Prophets and Kings (Tarikh al-Rusul wa-al-Muluk)

events.certainty_note: Abbasid administrative reforms are well-attested in later Islamic historiography, though precise dating of specific initiatives within the 750s–760s is sometimes uncertain. The general trajectory is secure.

events.title: Tang Dynasty Military Campaigns in Central Asia
events.description: The Tang Dynasty continues military operations to maintain control over the Silk Road and Central Asian territories. Following the Battle of the Talas River (751 CE), where Tang forces were defeated by the Abbasid Caliphate and Central Asian allies, Tang military presence in the region remains contested. By 760 CE, Tang forces are consolidating positions in the Tarim Basin and managing relationships with Turkic and Tibetan powers.
key_figures: An Lushan (military governor, recently deceased 757 CE)

sources.name: New Book of Tang (Xin Tang Shu), compiled 11th century
Records of the Grand Historian (Shiji) and continuations

events.certainty_note: Tang military activities in Central Asia are documented in Chinese chronicles, though specific campaigns in 760 CE are sometimes conflated with broader regional conflicts. The general pattern of Tang retrenchment after Talas is secure.

events.title: Frankish Expansion under Pepin III and Early Charlemagne
events.description: Pepin III (r. 751–768 CE) continues consolidation of Frankish power in Western Europe. His son Charles (later Charlemagne) is being groomed for succession and participates in military campaigns. The Frankish kingdom expands its influence over neighboring territories and strengthens ties with the Papacy, establishing the foundation for what will become the Carolingian Empire.

sources.name: Einhard, Life of Charlemagne (Vita Karoli Magni)

events.certainty_note: Frankish political developments are documented in Carolingian-era chronicles, though these were compiled after the events. The general trajectory of Pepin's reign and Charlemagne's early life is well-established.
cross_references: 768-001 (Pepin III's death and division of kingdom)

events.title: Byzantine-Arab Naval Conflicts in the Mediterranean
events.description: The Byzantine Empire under Constantine V (r. 741–775 CE) continues naval warfare against Arab forces in the Mediterranean. Byzantine fleets engage in raids and defensive operations against Umayyad and early Abbasid naval power. These conflicts are part of the broader struggle for control of Mediterranean trade routes and coastal territories.
key_figures: Arab naval commanders (names often unrecorded in Byzantine sources)
events.certainty_note: Byzantine-Arab naval conflicts are attested in Byzantine chronicles, but specific engagements in 760 CE are often undated or conflated with broader campaigns. The general pattern of ongoing Mediterranean conflict is secure.

disconfirming_evidence: The significance of specific events in 760 CE is difficult to assess because many sources conflate multiple years of the 750s–760s period. The Battle of Talas River (751 CE) is sometimes dated to 751 or 752 CE with scholarly disagreement. An Lushan's death is firmly dated to 757 CE, but the aftermath and Tang military reorganization in 760 CE are less precisely documented. No major disconfirming evidence contradicts the events listed, but their individual significance relative to broader trends is uncertain.
historiographic_note: Documentation for 760 CE is moderate but unevenly distributed. Islamic sources (later compilations like Tabari) provide detailed accounts of Abbasid administrative developments, though these were written centuries later and may reflect later institutional practices. Chinese chronicles offer good coverage of Tang military and political affairs, though specific campaigns are sometimes undated. Byzantine and Frankish sources are sparser for this year specifically, though the broader era is well-documented. The record is heavily weighted toward elite political and military history; economic, social, and cultural developments are underrepresented. Sub-Saharan Africa, Southeast Asia, and Northern Europe are largely absent from surviving written sources.

graph_edges.note: Abbasid consolidation under al-Mansur directly enabled the founding of Baghdad as the new capital
Tang retrenchment in Central Asia follows from the defeat at Talas River
Pepin III's consolidation of Frankish power set the stage for the division of his kingdom and Charlemagne's rise
Byzantine-Arab naval conflicts continued throughout Constantine V's reign
